items.q: Are monopolies always bad for consumers?
items.correct: Standard theory says monopolies restrict output and raise prices, but possible benefits include economies of scale, R&D investment and dynamic efficiency.
items.misconception: Monopolies are illegal and always harmful.
items.random_wrong: A monopoly is when one country dominates world trade.
items.why: Monopolies aren't automatically illegal — natural monopolies in utilities can be efficient. The analysis requires weighing static inefficiency against potential dynamic gains. Students treat the question as already settled.

items.correct: A tax set equal to the external cost of an activity — designed to internalise the externality so the private cost reflects the social cost. Used for pollution, congestion, sugary drinks.
items.misconception: A Pigouvian tax is a tax that funds government spending.
items.random_wrong: A Pigouvian tax is a tax only on rich people.
items.why: Pigouvian taxes are corrective, not revenue-raising. The aim is to align private incentives with social welfare. Students treat all taxes as the same (revenue), missing the distinct purpose of Pigouvian intervention.

items.q: What is the difference between GDP and GNP?
items.correct: GDP measures output produced within a country's borders; GNP measures output produced by a country's nationals wherever they are.
items.misconception: GDP and GNP are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: GDP is income and GNP is spending.
items.why: Foreign profits from a UK firm abroad count in UK GNP but not UK GDP. Profits of a Japanese firm in the UK count in UK GDP but Japan's GNP. The distinction matters for globalised economies.
The answer examiners want is: Rise on average. “Stay the same” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Consumer Prices Index. “Cheap Price Idea” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Last year basket £100, this year £105. Inflation %?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
